hypotheses.statement: R3F + Convex (BaaS) is the best stack for a pilot-proposal-level educational app with data persistence and teacher dashboard
R3F + Firebase is better because Firebase has documented children's privacy controls
testCriteria: Firebase children's privacy controls are COPPA-sufficient

hypotheses.statement: Self-hosted (PocketBase/SQLite) gives maximum control over data handling, enabling COPPA compliance by design
testCriteria: Self-hosting reduces vendor dependency for compliance
PocketBase provides auth + DB in single binary
architectureImplications: Full control over data residence and retention

scenarios.name: R3F + Convex Base: Managed BaaS with Auth
scenarios.description: R3F v9 for 3D rendering. Convex for backend (schema, queries, mutations). Convex Auth for teacher login. Student accounts managed by school. Dashboard as React pages with role-based views.
assumptions: A1: Convex provides SOC 2 compliance sufficient for EdTech
A2: Convex Auth integrates with school SSO (Clever/Google)
A3: COPPA school consent mechanism is legally valid for this use case
A4: Cost within budget for 100-500 students
contradictions: COPPA school consent conditions are unclear without legal review
Convex COPPA compliance posture not verified
governanceImplications: COPPA compliance depends on: (1) school consent mechanism validity, (2) Convex DPA, (3) privacy policy, (4) data retention policy — ALL UNVERIFIED
safetyImplications: Children's data stored on third-party cloud — breach liability
recommendedActions: Legal review of COPPA school consent
Convex DPA negotiation
Privacy policy drafting

evaluationScores.rationale: Convex COPPA posture not verified. School consent mechanism not confirmed with tier-1 evidence.
Convex + R3F is technically feasible, well-documented stack
COPPA violation = FTC enforcement action. Children's data breach = catastrophic.
Can switch BaaS but data migration + new DPA needed
Tech build 2-4 weeks, but legal review adds unknown timeline
GOVERNANCE IS THE CRITICAL FAILURE POINT. COPPA + FERPA compliance CANNOT be determined without legal review. Score 1 = non-compliant until verified.
R3F + Convex both mature stacks

scenarios.name: R3F + Convex Best: School Consent Works + SOC 2
scenarios.description: Legal review confirms school consent is valid. Convex provides DPA. Privacy policy approved. Teacher dashboard with per-student views. Adaptive difficulty via LEARN loop.
assumptions: A1: School consent mechanism validated by attorney
A2: Convex DPA meets school district requirements
A3: Privacy policy passes school/district review
A4: Pilot approved by district IT
governanceImplications: All compliance verified — but this is the BEST CASE assumption
safetyImplications: Data handling procedures documented and approved
recommendedActions: This is the target state — all prerequisites must be met first

evaluationScores.rationale: BEST CASE ONLY — none of these assumptions have evidence. Legal review has not occurred.
Same tech stack
If all compliance verified, risk is manageable — but evidence is zero
Same as base
Legal review timeline is unknown — could be weeks to months
Governance score cannot exceed 1 without completed legal review. This is an ASSUMPTION, not evidence.
Same

scenarios.name: R3F + Convex Worst: Legal Review Blocks Deployment
scenarios.description: Legal review reveals school consent mechanism is insufficient for this use case. Verifiable parental consent (VPC) required. VPC implementation adds months and cost. Pilot proposal rejected by district.
assumptions: A1: School consent mechanism does NOT cover commercial EdTech vendors without specific DPA
A2: District requires VPC for all under-13 data collection
A3: VPC implementation requires identity verification service ($$$)
A4: District IT rejects Convex as data processor without SOC 2 Type II + specific children's data addendum
architectureImplications: Architecture is fine — compliance blocks deployment
deploymentImplications: Cannot deploy until VPC + DPA resolved
governanceImplications: Project blocked by compliance. This is the REALISTIC scenario without legal preparation.
safetyImplications: Attempting deployment without compliance = FTC enforcement risk
recommendedActions: DO NOT deploy without completed legal review. This is not optional.

evaluationScores.rationale: District rejection of insufficient compliance is a documented pattern
VPC + DPA + legal review = months of non-technical work
FTC enforcement + district rejection = project death
Legal compliance work is not stack-specific — transferable
Blocked indefinitely by legal
Non-compliant — deployment blocked
Tech is fine — compliance is the blocker

scenarios.name: R3F + Convex Adversarial: Data Breach of Children's Records
scenarios.description: Third-party BaaS (Convex) suffers a data breach. Student names, scores, and session data exposed. FTC enforcement action. Parents sue. School district terminates contract. Developer personally liable.
assumptions: A1: BaaS providers are breach targets
A2: Children's data breach triggers enhanced FTC scrutiny
A3: Developer as COPPA operator bears personal liability
A4: School district has vicarious liability clauses in DPA
deploymentImplications: Insurance, incident response plan, breach notification procedures all required
governanceImplications: COPPA enforcement: FTC has imposed millions in fines for children's data violations
safetyImplications: Children's data exposure = maximum severity safety event
recommendedActions: Cyber liability insurance
Incident response plan
Data minimization

evaluationScores.rationale: Data breaches of children's data are documented (VTech, Edmodo, etc.)
Breach preparation is a full compliance program
CATASTROPHIC — FTC fines + lawsuits + career damage
Data breach cannot be reversed
Project terminated post-breach
Complete compliance failure
Irrelevant in breach scenario

scenarios.name: R3F + Firebase Base: Google Ecosystem with Children's Privacy
scenarios.description: R3F for 3D. Firebase (Firestore + Auth + Hosting). Firebase has documented children's privacy page. Google Workspace for Education SSO. Teacher dashboard as Firebase-hosted React app.
assumptions: A1: Firebase children's privacy controls satisfy COPPA
A2: Google Workspace for Education SSO is COPPA-compliant for school use
A3: Firestore security rules provide adequate row-level access control
A4: Firebase free tier (Spark) covers 100-500 students
contradictions: Firebase children's privacy page exists but specific COPPA compliance details need verification
Google/Firebase ToS may require developer to independently ensure COPPA compliance
governanceImplications: Firebase ToS likely states developer is responsible for COPPA — Firebase is processor, developer is operator. UNVERIFIED.
safetyImplications: Google infrastructure has strong security, but developer is still the COPPA operator
recommendedActions: Read Firebase children's privacy policy exactly
Verify Google Workspace for Education COPPA status
Legal review still required

evaluationScores.rationale: Firebase children's page exists but exact COPPA compliance posture not verified in this run
Firebase + R3F well-documented
Same COPPA risk as Option A — children's data is being stored
Google lock-in harder to reverse than Convex
Same timeline plus legal
SAME AS OPTION A: cannot score above 1 without legal review. Firebase having a children's page does not equal compliance.
Google/Firebase is the most mature BaaS

scenarios.name: Firebase Best: Google handles compliance complexity
scenarios.description: Firebase children's privacy controls + Google Workspace SSO + Firestore security rules provide a COPPA-compliant path. Google's legal infrastructure handles DPA with school districts.
assumptions: A1: Google provides standard DPA for education
A2: Firebase children's privacy page means Firebase assists with COPPA
A3: Google Workspace for Education is FTC-approved COPPA safe harbor
contradictions: Google's COPPA safe harbor status is for Google's OWN services, not for third-party apps using Firebase
governanceImplications: CRITICAL CONTRADICTION: Google's COPPA compliance covers Google products, NOT third-party apps built on Firebase. Developer remains the COPPA operator.
recommendedActions: Verify exact scope of Google's COPPA coverage for Firebase-based third-party apps

evaluationScores.rationale: Google's COPPA coverage for third-party Firebase apps is UNVERIFIED and likely does not extend to developer's app
Same
Slightly better than A if Google provides standard education DPA
Google lock-in
Legal review still required
Cannot score above 1. Google's compliance does not transfer to developer's app.
Most mature

scenarios.name: Firebase Worst: Firebase ToS makes developer sole COPPA operator
scenarios.description: Firebase ToS explicitly states developer is responsible for all regulatory compliance. Firebase is data processor only. Developer has no legal infrastructure for COPPA operator obligations.
assumptions: A1: Firebase ToS assigns COPPA operator responsibility to developer
A2: Individual developer cannot fulfill operator obligations without legal counsel
A3: District rejects pilot because developer cannot demonstrate COPPA operator compliance
deploymentImplications: Same as A-worst: blocked by compliance
governanceImplications: Developer as COPPA operator needs: privacy policy, VPC mechanism or school consent, data retention policy, breach notification procedure, data deletion capability
recommendedActions: Same as A-worst: legal review is prerequisite

evaluationScores.rationale: Firebase ToS typically assigns compliance to developer — well-known pattern
Compliance program creation for solo developer is extremely difficult
Same catastrophic risk
Compliance work transfers across stacks
Blocked by compliance
Non-compliant
Irrelevant

scenarios.name: Firebase Adversarial: Google changes pricing/ToS for children's data
scenarios.description: Google changes Firebase pricing or ToS for apps handling children's data. New requirements (e.g., mandatory COPPA audit, additional fees) make the project economically unviable.
assumptions: A1: Google has changed pricing before (Firebase pricing changes 2023)
A2: Google could add children's data handling fees
A3: Solo developer cannot absorb compliance cost increases
recommendedActions: Plan B for self-hosting
Budget for pricing changes

evaluationScores.rationale: Firebase pricing changes in 2023 are documented precedent
Migration is expensive
Economically unviable but not safety-critical
Google lock-in makes migration very expensive
Project may be abandoned
Compliance work transfers
Mature

scenarios.name: Self-Hosted (PocketBase) Base: Maximum Data Control
scenarios.description: R3F for 3D. PocketBase (Go binary) for backend: SQLite DB, built-in auth, REST API. Self-hosted on a VPS (Hetzner/DigitalOcean $5-10/mo). Teacher dashboard as React SPA. Full control over data residence.
assumptions: A1: PocketBase provides adequate auth for school-managed accounts
A2: Self-hosting gives full data control for COPPA compliance
A3: 1-2 devs can manage a single VPS
A4: PocketBase handles 100-500 concurrent users
contradictions: Self-hosting gives data control but 1-2 devs may not maintain security patches
architectureImplications: No vendor DPA needed (you ARE the vendor)
deploymentImplications: VPS management, backups, SSL, security patches — ongoing ops
governanceImplications: COPPA operator responsibilities are fully on developer. No vendor to share liability with. BUT: no vendor compliance gap either.
safetyImplications: Data security depends entirely on developer's ops practices
recommendedActions: Security audit of PocketBase deployment
Backup strategy
Still need legal review for COPPA compliance

evaluationScores.rationale: PocketBase for EdTech is not documented. Self-hosting gives control but compliance still unverified.
PocketBase is simple but self-hosting adds ongoing ops
Solo developer as data controller for children's data = maximum liability exposure
Standard SQLite — fully portable, no vendor lock-in
Self-hosted setup + legal review = longer timeline
SAME AS ALL OPTIONS: cannot score above 1 without legal review. Self-hosting doesn't solve COPPA — it just changes who is liable.
PocketBase is newer, smaller community

scenarios.name: Self-Hosted Best: Full control enables fastest compliance
scenarios.description: Self-hosting means no vendor DPA negotiation needed. Developer implements COPPA requirements directly: consent mechanism, data deletion API, retention limits. Legal review is simpler because there's no third party.
assumptions: A1: Legal review is simpler for self-hosted (no vendor dependency)
A2: Developer implements consent flow directly
A3: Data deletion API satisfies COPPA requirements
governanceImplications: No vendor dependency — but also no vendor security certifications to rely on
safetyImplications: Developer is sole guarantor of data security
recommendedActions: Legal review for self-hosted COPPA compliance

evaluationScores.rationale: Legal simplicity of self-hosting is INFERENCE — no evidence this is actually simpler for COPPA
Consent flow + deletion API + ops = significant work
No vendor breach risk, but developer breach risk remains
Standard stack, portable
Legal review + consent implementation
STILL 1 — no legal review has occurred
PocketBase newer

scenarios.name: Self-Hosted Worst: Security incident on unmanaged VPS
scenarios.description: Solo developer fails to apply security patch. VPS compromised. Children's educational records exposed. No incident response plan. No cyber insurance. Maximum personal liability.
assumptions: A1: Solo dev misses a critical security patch
A2: VPS lacks IDS/monitoring
A3: No incident response plan exists
A4: Developer has no cyber liability insurance
architectureImplications: Self-hosting + solo dev = weakest security posture
governanceImplications: Maximum personal liability — developer is controller AND processor
recommendedActions: If self-hosting: automated patching, IDS, backup encryption, cyber insurance

evaluationScores.rationale: Solo-managed VPS security incidents are well-documented
Security program for solo dev is extremely hard
WORST CASE: children's data breach + no insurance + personal liability
Breach is irreversible
Project destroyed
Total failure
Irrelevant

scenarios.name: Self-Hosted Adversarial: District IT rejects non-certified infrastructure
scenarios.description: School district IT department requires SOC 2 Type II or equivalent certification for any vendor storing student data. Self-hosted VPS with no certification is rejected. Pilot proposal dead.
assumptions: A1: District requires SOC 2 or equivalent
A2: Solo developer cannot obtain SOC 2
A3: District has standard vendor assessment process
safetyImplications: Not a safety issue — institutional process
recommendedActions: Check district vendor assessment requirements BEFORE choosing self-hosted

evaluationScores.rationale: District vendor assessment requirements are standard practice
SOC 2 certification is impossible for solo developer
Project rejected but no safety risk
Can switch to certified BaaS
Project blocked
Institutional requirement not met
Same

simulationRuns.result: 12 scenarios generated and scored. CRITICAL FINDING: ALL branches score 1/5 on Governance Compliance because COPPA/FERPA compliance CANNOT be determined without completed legal review. This is not a tech failure — it is a structural impossibility. The evaluator hard gate R9 (insufficient evidence) should REJECT any recommendation that claims compliance.
contradictionsFound: Firebase COPPA coverage scope (Google's compliance vs developer's compliance)
evidenceGaps: COPPA school consent mechanism exact conditions (needs legal review)
FERPA applicability to this specific EdTech scenario (needs legal review)
Firebase ToS exact COPPA operator assignment
District vendor assessment requirements for target schools
nextActions: Legal review is PREREQUISITE before ANY recommendation can be ACCEPTED

contradictionsAddressed: Firebase COPPA scope: clarified that Google's COPPA compliance covers Google products, NOT third-party Firebase apps. Developer is the COPPA operator regardless of BaaS choice.
revisionRounds.summary: Round 1: Firebase COPPA scope contradiction resolved — developer is COPPA operator regardless of BaaS. All branches have identical governance score (1/5) because the blocker is legal review, not technology choice.

contradictionsAddressed: Self-hosted compliance simplicity: remains INFERENCE. No evidence that self-hosting makes COPPA compliance simpler.
revisionRounds.summary: Round 2: Self-hosted compliance claim remains inference-grade. All 3 branches remain at Governance 1/5. 4 of 12 scenarios have evidenceSufficiency='insufficient'. 3 of 12 have contradictionStatus='detected'. Per scoring rubric stopping criteria: no scenarios can support a recommendation while governance is unresolved.
